title: 1 Chronicles 5
subtitle: Reuben, Gad, and the Half-Tribe of Manasseh

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — Reuben: the Firstborn Who Lost His Rights

refs.note: Jacob’s deathbed verdict on Reuben: “Turbulent as the waters, you will no longer excel, for you went up onto your father’s bed.”
Reuben slept with Bilhah, his father’s concubine. The Chronicler references this without narrating it.

notes.note: Reuben’s loss teaches that natural advantage does not guarantee spiritual inheritance. The firstborn who defiles his father’s bed loses everything. God’s gifts are not entitlements. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew states that the bĕkōrāh (“birthright”) was given to the sons of Joseph. This term covers the double inheritance portion (Deut 21:17), not the royal line. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Reuben was Jacob’s firstborn but lost the birthright because “he defiled his father’s marriage bed” (Gen 35:22). The rights of the firstborn were given to Joseph’s sons; the genealogical pre-eminence to Judah; the ruler came from Judah. The Chronicler explains all this in a single verse (v.1–2) — a masterclass in theological footnoting. The Reubenite genealogy extends to the Assyrian exile, with a note about territorial warfare against the Hagrites.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 11–26 — Gad and Manasseh; Exile for Unfaithfulness

heb.paragraph: The Transjordanian tribes were exiled because they were “unfaithful” (maʿal, v.25) to the God of their ancestors. This is the Chronicler’s key term for covenant violation — he uses it more than any other biblical author. Maʿal is not mere disobedience but betrayal: breaking faith with someone who trusted you.

refs.note: Tiglath-Pileser deported Galilee and the Transjordanian tribes. The Chronicler provides the theological explanation: unfaithfulness.
The Deuteronomistic verdict on Israel’s exile parallels the Chronicler’s but uses different vocabulary. Kings emphasises “sin”; Chronicles emphasises “unfaithfulness.”

notes.note: The Transjordanian tribes’ exile teaches that military strength provides no protection against covenant unfaithfulness. The same people who won battles through prayer lost everything through idolatry. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: wayyimʿălû — “they acted unfaithfully.” The qal of māʿal is used exclusively for covenant betrayal in Chronicles. The root appears 20 times in Chronicles compared to only 5 in all of Samuel-Kings. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Gad’s genealogy is brief, followed by the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan. The Chronicler records their military strength — 44,760 warriors — and their victory over the Hagrites (“because they cried out to God during the battle, and he answered their prayers because they trusted in him”, v.20). Then the devastating coda: “But they were unfaithful to the God of their ancestors and prostituted themselves to the gods of the peoples of the land... So the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria... and he took them into exile.” The genealogy that begins with military triumph ends in deportation.

notes.note: The devastating judgment recorded here was not arbitrary divine wrath but the long-warned consequence of persistent covenant violation. God had patiently sent prophet after prophet (2 Chr 36:15-16), but the people refused to repent. Believers must learn that God's patience, though immense, is not unlimited — He will discipline His people to restore them (Heb 12:5-11).

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The judgment of exile was not the end of God's purposes but a severe mercy designed to purify and restore. God disciplines those He loves (Heb 12:6). For the believer, seasons of divine discipline are not evidence of God's abandonment but of His fatherly care. He prunes every branch that bears fruit so that it will bear even more (John 15:2).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel (he was the firstborn, but when he defiled his father\u2019s marriage bed, his rights as firstborn were given to the sons of Joseph son of Israel; so he could not be listed in the genealogical record in accordance with his birthright,ESVThe sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel (for he was the firstborn, but because he defiled his father\u2019s couch, his birthright was given to the sons of Joseph the son of Israel, so that he could not be enrolled as the oldest son;NIVand though Judah was the strongest of his brothers and a ruler came from him, the rights of the firstborn belonged to Joseph)\u2014ESVthough Judah became strong among his brothers and a chief came from him, yet the birthright belonged to Joseph),

themes.note: Reuben, Gad, and the Half-Tribe of Manasseh

coaching.tip: The Transjordan tribes 'were unfaithful to the God of their ancestors and prostituted themselves to the gods of the peoples of the land.' The Chronicler explains their exile as a direct consequence. Every genealogical note in Chronicles carries a theological verdict.

prayer_prompt: The Transjordan tribes were conquered because they were unfaithful to God. Consider asking God about the connection between your spiritual faithfulness and your practical stability.
